Have you ever thought about why urgent care is flourishing so much in New York and what is the reason behind its success? Well, you might have not. [Urgent care in New York](https://nyucc.com/ "nyucc.com") offers a lot of facilities. The number of people visiting the urgent care center increases day by day due to the facilities they provide and the working efficiency they have. It is not an easy task to establish yourself as a safe and efficient institution. Urgent care has worked a lot to earn this much respect and loyalty from people.

1. Flexible working hours

The urgent care in New York provides flexible hours, unlike a doctor's private clinic that has rigid timings. The injuries do not occur with prior notice and therefore, in case of emergencies, it becomes necessary to visit the urgent care that provides services with immediacy. They offer you their medical services even during non-business hours.

2\. Save your money

The urgent cares help you save a lot of your money. They are much cheaper than visiting your primary doctor or emergency room. Therefore, if you are low on your budget, you can consider visiting the urgent care for sure.

3\. No appointments

To visit [urgent care in New York](https://nyucc.com/ "nyucc.com"), you do not need to book an appointment like you were required to do in case of visiting a clinic. The urgent cares save you from the frustration of appointments and are ready to serve you whenever you arrive. They make you feel comfortable, relaxed and treat you with full dedication.

[![Profile photo for Aaron Blight](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-thumb-696668759-50-yqyvftxvaoolvkyzeblyoikpdolxufsd.jpeg)](https://www.quora.com/profile/Aaron-Blight-1)

· [7y](https://www.quora.com/What-are-the-pros-and-cons-of-Starting-a-home-health-care-business/answer/Aaron-Blight-1)

If you’re going to start a home health care business, you need to be passionate about specifically helping seniors and their families.

If you’re considering this industry based primarily on numbers, money, and data, then you will find home health care to be an endless cycle of hassles and headaches.

Here are some of the PROS:

- You will make a difference in the lives of your clients and their families
- You learn a lot from your clients
- Demand for services is high
- The aging population means that demand will increase over time
- Societal focus on “aging in place” generates increased demand
- Relatively low bar

Continue Reading

If you’re going to start a home health care business, you need to be passionate about specifically helping seniors and their families.

If you’re considering this industry based primarily on numbers, money, and data, then you will find home health care to be an endless cycle of hassles and headaches.

Here are some of the PROS:

- You will make a difference in the lives of your clients and their families
- You learn a lot from your clients
- Demand for services is high
- The aging population means that demand will increase over time
- Societal focus on “aging in place” generates increased demand
- Relatively low barriers to entry
- Profitable margins are attainable
- Large private pay base of clients reduces delays in payment when compared to third party payers
- You will become known in your community
- Every day there’s a new challenge
- You will provide jobs to lots of people as your company grows
- Relatively little regulation compared to other healthcare industries
- It’s a people business

Here are some of the CONS:

- It’s a people business
- Regulatory oversight is going to continue to increase
- Margins are tightening
- Compared to other types of businesses at your level of revenue, you have an ENORMOUS number of employees to manage
- Staffing is a constant challenge
- You ALWAYS have employees working, 24/7/365
- Low barriers to entry enable competition to easily enter the market
- Alternate deli

Urgent care centers can be quite profitable. That is why they are “sprouting up like mushrooms”. They don’t accept Medicaid. Medicare doesn’t cover most of all of the cost either. That leaves these facilities with Insured patients and the uninsured where the expected fee is collected up front.

It is often quicker and cheaper for an insured patient to go to an urgent care clinic than the ER. The co-pay may be far less.

The prices are often cheaper. They are not trying to be all things to all people.

So they get paid for every patient. No they don’t have an orthopedic surgeon there or any specialis

Continue Reading

Urgent care centers can be quite profitable. That is why they are “sprouting up like mushrooms”. They don’t accept Medicaid. Medicare doesn’t cover most of all of the cost either. That leaves these facilities with Insured patients and the uninsured where the expected fee is collected up front.

It is often quicker and cheaper for an insured patient to go to an urgent care clinic than the ER. The co-pay may be far less.

The prices are often cheaper. They are not trying to be all things to all people.

So they get paid for every patient. No they don’t have an orthopedic surgeon there or any specialist other than ER medicine and perhaps an internist. If you suspect a heart attack you need an ER. A simple laceration they will repair. A non-displaced fracture they will splint and have you follow up with an orthopedic surgeon. Bursitis, they will inject you with cortisone.

They needn’t be busy if they are getting paid for every patient well above costs. In fact many insurance companies suggest urgent care centers. These are cherry picking those who really don’t need an emergency room and have insurance. Specialty surgical hospitals do the exact same thing. No ER at those hospitals.

What is the difference between going to the hospital as an urgent care patient and being admitted as an inpatient?

[Mark Johnson](https://www.quora.com/profile/Mark-Johnson-2431)

Retired Internal Medicine MD 1981 UCSF, UCDavis , Kaiser  · Author has 20.1K answers and 16M answer views

· [3y](https://www.quora.com/What-is-the-difference-between-going-to-the-hospital-as-an-urgent-care-patient-and-being-admitted-as-an-inpatient/answer/Mark-Johnson-2431)

People go to hospitals often the ED attached to it with no prior appointment. Urgent care is a separate part of Care as well, usually given appointments by an advice nurse by telephone .

The degree of illness severity in theory decides whether to go to the ED vs Urgent care.

Being admitted to hospital means the patient was seen either place or in the clinic and felt to be sick enough to require hospitalization by their severity illness. Or they are being admitted for elective surgery. Patients from a different hospital are sometimes transferred by air or land ambulance to a higher level hospital

Continue Reading

People go to hospitals often the ED attached to it with no prior appointment. Urgent care is a separate part of Care as well, usually given appointments by an advice nurse by telephone .

The degree of illness severity in theory decides whether to go to the ED vs Urgent care.

Being admitted to hospital means the patient was seen either place or in the clinic and felt to be sick enough to require hospitalization by their severity illness. Or they are being admitted for elective surgery. Patients from a different hospital are sometimes transferred by air or land ambulance to a higher level hospital. Hospitals are rated by how severe of illness they are staffed for. In particular not all hospitals have a neurosurgeon on duty at all times. So a serious head injury may be life flighted to the larger hospital that does have a neurosurgeon. The word ‘ inpatient’ vs ‘outpatient’ refers to whether that person is sick enough to be admitted to the hospital.

Outpatients are people who see their doctors in the regular clinic. However even those people may be sick enough to be admitted or transferred to a hospital that can properly take care of them. People do not know our internal systems if triage . They do not always present with their full blown illness at first, thinking this illness is not too serious when in fact it is deadly. Physicians and nurses are trained to evaluate severity of illness and do in fact triage people to

Nothing. I was a Primary Care Internal Medicine doctor for 31 years and 23 years at Kaiser. I was a Chief Resident. I was Associate Clinical Professor of UCDavis Medical School and did rounds and teaching residents and students for 13 years . In our system all the Primary Care Doctors had to help out in Evening Urgent Care Clinic one evening per week in lieu of being Internist on call in the Hospital all night or all day for 12 our shifts. When Hospitalists took over we who chose to not be Hospitalists did our share of on call or urgent care once a week.

Perhaps you are asking about doctors who

Nothing. I was a Primary Care Internal Medicine doctor for 31 years and 23 years at Kaiser. I was a Chief Resident. I was Associate Clinical Professor of UCDavis Medical School and did rounds and teaching residents and students for 13 years . In our system all the Primary Care Doctors had to help out in Evening Urgent Care Clinic one evening per week in lieu of being Internist on call in the Hospital all night or all day for 12 our shifts. When Hospitalists took over we who chose to not be Hospitalists did our share of on call or urgent care once a week.

Perhaps you are asking about doctors who choose to only do urgent care day or evening or attached to an ED?

I never thought something was “ wrong”. These doctors are often trying to avoid the responsibilities of having a large panel and value their home time by not having to do computer emails to patents for 2–3 hours every day. They want shift work and less overtime and often have family responsibilities or children or someone ill at home.

Many Doctor jobs are shift work: anesthesiology, radiology, urgent care , hospitalist, emergency room doctors, People have a right to chose their life and their salary may be less but they are happier or it fits their home life better. Many Anesthesiologists like to work for 6 months on an travel the world for six mont

Urgent care clinics treat many non-life‑threatening acute problems but are not equipped or staffed for everything. Common things they typically do not handle:

Immediate life‑threatening emergencies

- Chest pain with suspected heart attack, severe shortness of breath, stroke symptoms (face droop, arm weakness, speech difficulty), major trauma, severe bleeding, or altered consciousness — these require 911/ER.

High-acuity hospital care

- Intensive care, continuous cardiac monitoring for unstable patients, ventilator support, complex hemodynamic management, and other critical‑care interventions.

Major trau

Continue Reading

Urgent care clinics treat many non-life‑threatening acute problems but are not equipped or staffed for everything. Common things they typically do not handle:

Immediate life‑threatening emergencies

- Chest pain with suspected heart attack, severe shortness of breath, stroke symptoms (face droop, arm weakness, speech difficulty), major trauma, severe bleeding, or altered consciousness — these require 911/ER.

High-acuity hospital care

- Intensive care, continuous cardiac monitoring for unstable patients, ventilator support, complex hemodynamic management, and other critical‑care interventions.

Major trauma and surgical emergencies

- Compound fractures with exposed bone, major open wounds requiring operative repair, severe burns (especially >10% TBSA, any airway burn, or full‑thickness burns), crushed limbs, and injuries needing immediate orthopedic or neurosurgical intervention.

Complex diagnostic needs

- Advanced imaging beyond X‑ray and basic CT (e.g., MRI for acute spinal cord compression may be unavailable), specialized cardiology testing (full echocardiography, stress testing with imaging), invasive diagnostics, or rapid access to specialist consults.

Procedures requiring operating room or anesthesia

- Emergency surgeries, procedures requiring general anesthesia, complex wound repairs needing plastic surgery, and endoscopic procedures (EGD, colonoscopy).

Obstetrics and high‑risk pregnancy care

- Labor and delivery, fetal distress, ectopic pregnancy requiring emergent surgery, and management of significant obstetric hemorrhage.

Pediatric critical care and neonatology

- Neonatal resuscitation beyond basic stabilization, severe pediatric respiratory distress, and other pediatric conditions needing pediatric ICU.

Complex infectious disease managem

“Urgent” in urgent care means the problem needs prompt attention but is not an emergency requiring immediate life‑saving care. Several predictable constraints make waits longer than the word suggests:

Why waits happen

- Variable demand: Urgent care centers see unpredictable surges (after-hours, evenings, weekends, flu season, post-holiday injuries). A cluster of patients with similar complaints creates queues.
- Limited resources: Fixed number of clinicians, rooms and nurses. A single provider must evaluate, treat, document, and often supervise procedures; each encounter takes time.
- Triage and priorit

Continue Reading

“Urgent” in urgent care means the problem needs prompt attention but is not an emergency requiring immediate life‑saving care. Several predictable constraints make waits longer than the word suggests:

Why waits happen

- Variable demand: Urgent care centers see unpredictable surges (after-hours, evenings, weekends, flu season, post-holiday injuries). A cluster of patients with similar complaints creates queues.
- Limited resources: Fixed number of clinicians, rooms and nurses. A single provider must evaluate, treat, document, and often supervise procedures; each encounter takes time.
- Triage and prioritization: Clinics triage arrivals. Patients with potentially more serious conditions are seen before those with minor problems, so low‑acuity cases can wait.
- Diagnostic time: Some visits require point‑of‑care tests (rapid strep, COVID, urinalysis), X‑rays, stitches, or intravenous meds. Tests and imaging introduce unavoidable delays.
- Contagion control and cleaning: Rooms and equipment need cleaning between patients, and infection‑control protocols (PPE changes, air turnover) add time.
- Administrative work: Registration, insurance verification, medication reconciliation, prescription processing and referrals all consume staff time.
- Staffing variability: Many clinics run with minimal after‑hours staff; unexpected sick calls or physician consulting needs slow throughput.
- Complexity and unpredictability: A patient may present with vague symptoms that require longer evaluation, phone consultations, or observation; such cases lengthen the queue for everyone.

What “urgent” implies operationally

- Timely, not instantaneous: Urgent care aims to treat non‑life‑threatening conditions sooner than a primary care appointment, and faster and

Unfortunately, over the last twenty years, there has been an explosion of NPs being trained by online degree mills, after only a one year intense RN program and very limited clinical experience. Continue Reading

The majority of urgent cares in America hire Nurse Practitioners as their providers, as a cost saving move over hiring physicians.

Unfortunately, over the last twenty years, there has been an explosion of NPs being trained by online degree mills, after only a one year intense RN program and very limited clinical experience. While there are excellent NPs out there, the training is so variable that you never know what you are getting with NPs. Compared to MDs in the US, who all have a core level of training that is pretty equivalent.

They fit into the US healthcare, because since corporations imposed the 15 minute appointment in most primary care offices, doctors are overbooked and can rarely get patients in that day for urgent problems, and most of those problems are too trivial for an ER. Urgent cares are to take those problems like cuts and strep throat and UTIs that used to go to primary care, but can’t get in now.

Life-threatening emergencies, such as a heart attack or serious head injury, require a visit to the emergency department, also called the emergency room (ER). An illness or injury that does not appear to be life threatening but can’t wait until the next day should be treated at an urgent care center.

Whether you are creating a new urgent care center from raw space in a strip mall or office building or refurbishing an existing medical practice space, careful planning and near-flawless implementation are required to achieve a build- out that will satisfy patients and meet your business objectives.

You may have a good idea of what you want to accomplish as you build out an urgent care center. However, before you begin, it’s important to review your goals and create an explicit, effective process and plan to successfully develop an urgent care center.

How much it cost?

The cost to open an urgent care varies based on market, goals, and available resources.

Starting up an urgent care begins with research, followed by careful planning and development, and finally, a leap of faith. The cost depends on important variables like location, size, and your real estate arrangement.

Clinic Location: Prime real estate may be easy to spot, but it’s not always easy to come by. Whether you’re planning to buy, build, or lease clinical space, opening your urgent care in the right location is crucial.

Highly visible, high-traffic, easily accessible retail space may require a bigger initial budget, but in the long run, it may pay off with a greater flow of patients.

Clinic Size: The average urgent care is about 3,000 square feet, but

Is Urgent Care cheaper than ER?

[![Profile photo for Amy Chai](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-thumb-72497003-50-orecthzbcjjwvnwwlxzauthsihxfpzlj.jpeg)](https://www.quora.com/profile/Amy-Chai-3)

· [6y](https://www.quora.com/Is-Urgent-Care-cheaper-than-ER/answer/Amy-Chai-3?no_redirect=1)

Not if you go to the urgent care with a condition that REALLY should have gone to the ER. (Chest pain, abdominal pain, complex laceration, etc)

It WILL be cheaper if you just need a tetanus shot or a strep test or something.

If you go to the urgent care thinking to avoid a wait and get charged less, but your visit is actually an emergency (or serious beyond what they can do at an urgent care) then you might end up paying for 1-the urgent care; 2-an ambulance; and 3-the ER. In that case, it is much more costly. And the UC doctor will NOT want the liability of you driving yourself to the ER if the

Continue Reading

Not if you go to the urgent care with a condition that REALLY should have gone to the ER. (Chest pain, abdominal pain, complex laceration, etc)

It WILL be cheaper if you just need a tetanus shot or a strep test or something.

If you go to the urgent care thinking to avoid a wait and get charged less, but your visit is actually an emergency (or serious beyond what they can do at an urgent care) then you might end up paying for 1-the urgent care; 2-an ambulance; and 3-the ER. In that case, it is much more costly. And the UC doctor will NOT want the liability of you driving yourself to the ER if there is any question that you might pass out or something while driving to the ER.

Urgent care clinics often have xrays, they can splint a sprain, they can treat strep throat, they can do a pregnancy test, they can repair small lacerations. They are NOT a miniature ER. NOT.

For example, if you have bad abdominal pain, the “worst headache of your life” or something like that, chances are good you will be getting a CT scan and some blood work. They don’t have that in the UC. Just go to the ER and avoid the hassle. If you are having chest pain and you are worried about your heart, just call 911.

The urgent care is for minor problems, not legitimate emergencies. That is why they call it a “walk-in” clinic and not a “drag-in” clinic.
